content:
Member-only story

APIs aren’t always needed

4 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

We’ve all been there. You start a fresh backend project, and it feels amazing. The code is clean, the endpoints are fast, and everything just works.
But fast forward six months. That cute little project has turned into an absolute monster.
You open up the codebase, and suddenly you’re staring at a mess:

• You have API endpoints that nobody actually calls — they just exist so a cron job can ping them to start a background task.

• You’ve got heavy scheduling logic buried deep inside your application code.

• Queues, background workers, and controllers are all tangled up together like cheap headphones.

• One massive service is trying to handle web traffic, process files, and send emails all at the same time.
Before you know it, deploying a tiny typo fix takes fifteen minutes. Your cloud bill is creeping up because your servers are running 24/7 just waiting around for work. Trying to scale the app feels like playing Jenga in an earthquake.
How did we get here? We got here because as developers, our muscle memory is always to build an API. Got a new requirement? “Oh, just spin up a new controller and…
